sentences.text: Yes.
In 1944, we were all-- well, the Russians were coming nearer, and they wanted to evacuate everybody from the ghetto.
And there was all this rush.
Everybody should-- should go.
We should-- we will be going to Germany.
They were going around with these loudspeakers, saying just come freely.
You will be well-treated.
You will get work, and you will get paid, and you will have plenty of food and everything.
Just come freely, and you will all be all right.
Of course, most of the people didn't believe this.
We have had already so many experiences.
Me and my sister decided-- by this time we were just the two of us-- that we're going to hide.
So we went up into a very old building, and right on the top, there was-- on the-- on the loft, very dark, there was one opening.
So my sister made sure, first of all, I should be hidden.
So she put me into this vestibule.
She says, go and lie down.
But when I laid down, there was a couple lying in there already.
And if I laid next to them, my feet would stick out, because it was just such a-- so I moved in as much as I could, with my feet in-- in, which I could.
But just the same, what shall I tell you?
But my sister couldn't get in anymore.
I still managed, with crumpling my feet up like this.
But my sister couldn't get in.
So she was lying in a-- in another area of that-- of the-- of the loft.
We could hear the Germans coming up the wooden stairs.
You can hear them with these boots, you know?
And then with the lights going around, like this.
And sure enough, we could hear everybody going down, already being rounded up, going down the stairs.
And there I was.
I didn't want to come out straight away because of the couple.
If I-- if I would have come out there, they would have seen that there is another couple lying there, and they were already hidden.
But I knew my sister must've been gone, because I heard everybody walking down.
In any case, I wasn't going to be left completely alone up there with this couple.
So I started walking down.
But when I came down, it was like a ghost coming down.
I was very thin, you know.
And they looked-- where did I come from-- because they went around with the-- with the torch to see, you know?
Did I look a state.
Apparently, you know, in that corner, there were feathers, and my hair was full of-- I looked at myself [INAUDIBLE] dirty, you know.
Well, I came down, and they had one look at me.
And somehow or other, they gave me just a push, and that's all.
And I-- I managed to get down.
And soon enough, I saw my sister, and we got together, and this is how we went into the trains.
We went into these closed trains, which, of course, you heard about, which they packed in so many people, like herrings.
And there was no air.
The little windows on the top, tiny, little openings on the top.
Dark, you couldn't see anything.
And how I survived this journey, I really don't know.
The smell, and there were just so many people, and it was terrible.
No water, nothing to eat, nothing.
And there we were, in this-- this train.
And we were going, travelling, and I had such cramps in my stomach.
I didn't know what happened to me.
It was terrible.
Anyway, suddenly, we stopped.
And one fellow went on top of the other and looked out of the little window to see where we are.
And one-- he says, it looks to us-- he looks-- he looked again-- and he said, he thinks it's not people.
There are no people around here.
They are monkeys.
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS]
So anyway, then suddenly, they came.
Raus, raus, with the dogs, with the rifles, so many of them.
And they started straight away-- raus, and straight away, made selections.
So it was, straight away, older people, children, and people who they thought was not good for work.
Each one was different areas.
We didn't know where we were going.
I was with my sister.
Now, I was carrying-- because they told us we should bring everything with us-- I was carrying another coat, because in case it's going to be cold, I'll need another coat.
And-- because clothes, in the ghetto, we had plenty, because it came back all from-- from, you know.
So the Gemeinde, you know, from the-- gave out clothes for people.
So that we had.
But of course, the food we didn't have, you know?
Anyway.
So I was carrying this coat, and I carried a suitcase noch [INAUDIBLE] with me.
And I was-- we were, walking, and we were stopping.
And such a long journey, it was, this walk.
And it was-- we arrived in August.
It was hot.
I had one coat on me and one coat-- I felt like throwing everything away, but I-- I-- you know, where would you throw it?
And this is how we arrived, eventually, after hours of walking and stopping.
We thought they were killing us on the way, I think.
We arrived to one place, and the first thing they did, we had to take everything off.
Everything went on a heap.
The clothes which we had, we had to undress.
Everything went completely.
And of course, we had to be completely naked.
I carried all this for what?
Anyway, and then they started shaving the hair, and they shaved us everywhere, you know, and put such a horrible, smelly disinfectant, which was burning like hell.
And now, we said, what is happening now?
We didn't know what's going to happen to us.
And then eventually, they gave us a piece of soap.
We are trying, now, to see if the soap is real, you know.
Then we came into an enormous place, which had all the showers coming down, but we didn't know whether this is going to be gas or shower-- or water.
Sure enough, when we saw the water coming down, we knew that we are OK.
And this is how we came-- we came to Birkenau.
No.
I never heard of it.
Maybe some people even heard about it.
I was so-- I didn't know.
I was ill.
I was involved with this work.
I could hardly survive my own life.
I didn't know anything about Auschwitz.
Here and there, you could hear something, but you didn't know what to hear, what-- what was the truth?
You didn't know.
In the ghetto, no.
I didn't know.
Yes.
When we arrived, people were already saying, let's hope it's not [INAUDIBLE].
People already sort of knew a little bit.
But then we got frightened, and especially if they tell us to undress completely.
I had a pair of shoes, which were still all right, so they took them off me.
If you had tattered shoes, they left you.
So straight away, I was running around and going around in mud, there.
You don't know what Birkenau was like when we were there.
Now, you can't recognize it.
If you go now, you don't see the same Auschwitz, because now it's growing grass or something, I heard.
We were walking in the deepest mud when it was raining, barefoot. 'Til here in mud, we were walking.
It was terrible.
Cold.
I don't know how-- do you know how strong the human being is-- are?
You can't imagine.
Because when I think that I was so ill, and I was-- I was walking around.
They give us a rag.
Everyone got off there with a rag, that's-- one rag.
Nothing else.
No underwear, no cardigans, no pullovers, no nothing, no shoes.
And--
Like hell on earth, really hell on earth.
Now, when this man went up and looked down and said that these are monkeys, you could just imagine why, because they were all wearing these black rags, you know, or any rags.
They were all stooping, walking like this.
No hair, because their hair was shaved.
And they looked like animals.
They didn't look like people, because they were just sort of stooping, going down like that.
We couldn't believe it when we saw what's happening.
Anyway, I would have been with my sister not-- I wouldn't be very long with my sister in Auschwitz, because there was soon a selection.
I don't know how long after, whether one week or two weeks or three.
There was a selection, and we were in fives.
Our-- being in Birkenau was only because as long-- if they will-- might need us.
Because we-- it was 1944, and in August, and we didn't have-- they didn't get any numbers.
It was only a question whether we will be gassed or whether they will need us for work, because they still needed people for work.
So the question was now-- so we were standing on [INAUDIBLE].
Every morning, 6:00 in the morning till 12:00, we were being counted.
We were standing in fives and being counted, like sheep.
And we had one to count us.
This was Irma Grese.
She was a real sadist.
She was very beautiful, but ice cold beautiful, if you know what I mean.
Blond and blue eyes, but ice cold.
She was always walking around with dogs, and-- big dogs.
And she always had like a [INAUDIBLE], you know, to-- to hit you with-- to hit you with.
God forbid if somebody didn't stand straight, or something.
It was-- well, anyway, [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] and I would have been selected with my sister, because we were in fives.
They told us to stand in fives, outside the barracks.
Because between the barracks, there was enough-- was exactly the same-- let's say, there is a barrack, and there is a space exactly as wide as the barrack.
And then again, a barrack and exactly the same width of a barrack.
That was for us to stand to be counted on.
So as we were-- were called out to stand in fives, I went through with my sister.
And I thought, now I'll be out of Birkenau, of Auschwitz.
But it didn't take long, because as we went across the lines to go to the other side of Birkenau, where the trains were supposed to take us to Germany, they didn't like the selection.
They saw that some people were not good enough for the work-- for work.
So they started, again, making a new selection, ones.
And as we were in one, my sister, who was-- who was not as thin as I was-- she tried to-- she was in front of me, but she wanted to sort of hide-- to hide me.
The Germans came over, divided us, and sent my sister to work, and me back away with all this-- with-- sorry to say, was-- was not good for work, with cripples.
And sure enough, I-- I thought now, that's the end of me.
I'll never come out of this hell.
And my sister was crying.
And a few girls-- they sent us to a cellar, which was like a garden room.
Half the window was in-- inside and half on top.
It was quite a dark room.
And some of the girls opened the windows, and they ran out.
But I was just sitting down.
I didn't want to do anything, because I was afraid.
At that point, I wasn't so strong, and I wasn't healthy.
They can-- they can kill me if they-- if they get hold of me.
Anyway, after about four hours in there, we had no-- nothing to drink, nothing to eat, and it was so hot in August.
They got us to stand in fives again and marched us to the gates of Birkenau, but we were outside the gates of Birkenau.
Because [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] now the gates were closed, and we are standing outside, and we didn't know what they were going to do with us.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] We had to stand like soldiers, with no-- nothing to drink.
My mouth was-- all of us-- dry, and the sun was really burning our heads, no-- no-- no-- shaved heads.
Anyway, we had two German women who were in charge of us.
They were going in and out, and until they went, until they came out-- hours were-- we were standing and-- and not knowing still what they're going to do with us.
It was getting dusk, they opened the gates, and they let us in.
There was no crematorium going that day.
If the crematorium would be going that day, well, we wouldn't be here.
I wouldn't be here.
And this happened to me.
This was the first time.
This was when I was separated with my sister.
My sister never believed, ever, that she will ever see me alive.
But I had hoped that, because she was sent to work, that I might see her.
But anyway, we-- on another selection-- it was past another few weeks and I wasn't selected.
I was put in a dark room in the corner of the barrack there, in-- in Birkenau.
And I didn't know what's going to happen to us now.

It so happens it was 1944, and by that time, whether it was September, October-- maybe middle of September or something-- that the crematoriums were going, still going, but certain days, yes, certain.
And in any case, they weren't going to-- to do the-- the gas for-- unless they had a big transport.
Well, anyway, in the evening, we were sitting there, we didn't know-- we were standing in there.
We didn't know what was going to happen to us.
And then they opened up the door and let us out again, and we could mix with all the people in the barrack.
I couldn't believe it that this is happening to me.
But when this happened the third time, another couple of weeks had went by, and every time, Josef Mengele, with his entourage, they arrived on one side of the barrack, walked in.
And they would say, this side, everybody stand up.
This side, everybody kneel.
And they would be doing, maybe, this side at first.
And when he came out a third time-- by this time, I had met some cousins.
And I said to my cousin-- they asked me about my sister and I said, my sister has been sent away to work.
And when she-- they heard everything what happened to me, they said, you have to stay with us.
We were holding on by our hands, not to go anywhere.
When they came to-- to do the selection, my cousins went through, but me, they pushed me this side.
I am no.
So when I could see this is happening three times already, I said, I can't be always having this luck.
So at that moment-- my cousins were already out-- I quickly took my rag in my hand.
And, you know, the barrack was divided in the middle.
It had like a-- like a little wall like this from bricks.
So I went on top of that-- that-- when I could see whether I was watching, of course, I went across to the other side and kneeled down, because the others were not being selected.
So at this time, with the people who were not being selected.
And I went across to this-- and kneeling down, I went as far as the door.

And when she was letting through quite a few people from one side, I-- I was so thin-- I quickly took off, because then-- I had my rag on when I was kneeling-- quickly took my rag on and went through.
She was standing like this.
I went-- I went through.
She was letting through this way.
I went through.
But now, I was afraid.
If they see me alone, what is going to happen?
So there were four girls.
They were always standing in fives, but one foursome was standing, and one girl said to me, come and stand with us.
We are only four.
So I quickly went across and I stood with them, and I managed to get through.
And this is how I saved my life.
And then I thought I would be with my cousins, but the train was-- was being divided into different places.
The girl who told me to come and stay with us, [?
Isrella, ?] and she is the girl who lives in Tel Aviv.
And we've been friends-- well, we're like sisters-- all our lives.
We always in contact.
And I was looking for my cousins, but my cousins weren't there.
Would I have gone with my cousins, if I had the luck, I would have been all right, because my cousins were-- are working in the kitchen.
And the best place to be in-- in-- in-- in the camps was to be in the kitchen.
At least, whatever it was, you had another potato or something.
There was no luxury, but survival.
There was a potato or whatever.
And this is how we were sent.
Then of course, we were sent by trains-- normal trains, you know-- to Germany.
The German trains, we were sent to.
That was when I-- when I managed to get out-- well said, fourth time, practically, because three times, they didn't select me.
But I went out.
At that time, they sent us to Oederan, to Ger-- to German and near Chemnitz, to a working camp.
We arrived there, it was winter time.
We must have arrived-- I don't know exactly the month.
Maybe November, October.
October, November, we arrived in Oederan.
And in Oederan, we were in-- like in a prison.
They had these iron bars all over, and we weren't allowed to go near the window.
Only a meter away from the window, because we weren't allow-- allowed to look down, what is going on downstairs.
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS]
And [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] in Oederan it was very, very difficult.
They brought us there because there was a-- ammunition-- there were ammunition factories.
I mean, when I say factories, where they produced ammunition, the machinery of these-- of the ammunition.
They had, like, not a factory, but a-- big places where they kept the-- the-- like lager, you know, for-- for-- where they kept-- what would you call that?
The machinery where they operated to make, for the guns, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] the ammunition.
For the ammunition.
And some-- some machinery were operated automatically, and some had to be with a-- with a handle, like the very old way.
They were very hard to operate.
Anyway, when I-- when I got there, I got very, very-- I was already very ill before.
And two girls died in this place of tuber-- of tuberculosis.
But they couldn't eat, and every time, they said, send it to Minia.
Because they had so much pity for me, I was so thin.
They couldn't eat anymore.
And I didn't want it, because I knew they need it.
But no, they were already really dying already, they knew they were, and they didn't want it, and they couldn't eat.
So they sent it to me so I should survive.
And one night, [?
Chanke, ?] she was such a beautiful girl.
These two girls, they were so sad-- I mean, the Germans were so sadistic.
They left some girls with long hair, and some-- and most of us were shaved, so that we can see what somebody looks like with hair.
And this girl, this [?
Chanke, ?] had this most beautiful, natural hair, till here, beautiful girl.
And she had-- she got pneumonia, actually, and she didn't look-- it didn't-- well, there was no medicine.
There was nothing there.
And one day, she developed a very, very high temperature.
Then they isolated her, put her into another place, because she was very contagious.
And one night-- and I dreamt about her that night, it's really amazing.
I don't know how it was.
In the morning, they told me that [?
Chanke ?] died.
It's amazing, things which were happening.
And the other girl was coughing and coughing.
She was also very ill.
This was-- And in 1944, in February, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] we were sent to-- on the open trains.
We were going-- one morning, we started very early, travelling all day.
And then we would be travelling all night.
We came back on the same place where we started.
They let us out down in the fields, just to do our business.
And we never knew what they were going to do with us.
And then after I don't know how many days-- whether it was a week or ten days, I don't know exactly-- they let us down, because we were on the Czech border.
And they marched us into Theresien-- Theresien-- Theresienstadt.
And there we stayed two-- two months until we were liberated by the Russians.
But I was very ill at that time.
I was-- we were standing for this soup, and I was having a couple of spoons of soup, and then suddenly, I collapsed.
I didn't know what happened to me.
They-- the next thing I knew, I was lying on a wooden [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] bridge, you know-- bunk.
And they were feeding me with salt, pieces of salt, and calcium, which looked like chalk.
It really didn't look like calcium.
It was chalk, it looked like.
And there was so much dysentery and so much illness, and they had all the rooms-- the houses-- were all made into hospitals, because the real hospital was full.
Until at the very end, they-- it was already-- I think it must have been in end of April-- that they sent me to the hospital.
And-- and that was my savior, when I went to the hospital.
They didn't have any medicine, but there was one doctor.
And he took-- his-- his-- upon himself to look after, to see everybody, how people-- you know, to-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] how do you say?
To-- to see how they are, you know?
And he came to my bed, and he saw I'm all alone, have nobody there.
So he said-- he asked me my name, and I said to him.
And he said, do I know from anybody from the family?
I said no, I haven't heard of anybody.
And then he-- he had a machine in that-- in this-- in the hospital, they had a very old-fashioned looking-through machine, not-- not X-rays, like you've got now.
That didn't exist.
They only had one, which they could look through the lungs, just, to see-- such a machine, a [INAUDIBLE].
I don't know what it was called.
Anyway, he looked at me.
He told me to undress and to look at my chest.
And he was shaking his head.
He was very upset because he said to me, you cannot travel anywhere.
You can't go anywhere.
You can't back-- go back to Poland.
You can't stay here, and you won't survive being here.
And he wanted to tell me that there's a transport going to England, but he didn't know how to say it to me, because I had tuberculosis.
It's very dan-- it's very contagious.
Anything else, he's have straight away told me.
So first of all, he told me, every morning, I must come up for one hour and go for a walk, and rest the rest of the time.
The same in the afternoon.
And he made sure that I get a bit more soup than everybody else.
Which nationality was he?
From Vienna.
He was from Vienna, Jewish.
And he, after a few weeks-- every time, every week, he came to see me, sometimes twice a week.
And then after a while, he came to me, he said-- he called me Mina.
Mina, there is a transport going to England, and I want you to be on this list.
I tell you, that somebody said that to me-- I-- you could have said to me anything but this, I didn't believe such a thing should happen.
He said to me, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] you must go so-and-so.
He told me where to go to get myself put on the list.
He said I must not say I'm ill, [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] and I must not-- and I mustn't say I live in the hospital, but I am living-- he gave me an address.
When I came there, it was such a long walk to go, and I was so weak.
Theresienstadt.
And I went to this place where you're supposed to put your name down, and when I came, there-- he had-- the one who was in charge, and there were others, they had one look at me.
They said, but you're ill.
The first thing.
You-- you've been-- you've come from the hospital, you look so very ill.
So I said, no, I'm not ill, and I live here and here and here.
They said no, it's not good enough, you must bring me a certificate from the doctor.
So I went back, and that took a while, again, because he couldn't give it to me straight away.
Then he gave me a certificate, and I've got this certificate somewhere.
You know, I wish I could find it.
Actually, I've looked for it.
I've got it somewhere-- where he says, I am [? lausefrei. ?] I am free of-- of-- of [? debt, ?] you know, so to say.
Lausefrei, ?] it's written on it.
I went with this to-- why I haven't got it, because when I went back with this, there, they said, this is not good enough.
They don't want that.
They want a proper certificate from a doctor that I am not contagious.
And for  him to write this was really something, but he took it upon himself.
And this, I gave them.
That's why I haven't got it.
But this other one, [? lausefrei, ?] he gave me first.
And then I was put on the list, and then I was sent to live together into a kindergarten, you know, like a nurs-- because children were small until 16, supposed to be.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And--
No.
No, no, they just accept-- he said, he gave-- he wrote all the details already, the doctor, and told them.
And he told me what to say, anyway.
I mean, I had to do everything what he said, because otherwise, I wouldn't be put on the list.
Yes, 'til 16.
Anyway.
So anyway, what happened now was that I was sent to the kindergarten.
I was together with all the girls, and there, we had a little bit better food, as well, you know, in that place.
And now, we were waiting for the planes to come to pick us up from England.
And we were sent for one month to Prague.
And in Prague, one day, the planes arrived.
There were 12 planes-- bomber planes.
Of course, there were no bombs in it.
And-- from the RAF.
And we arrived to Carlisle Airport, which is a military airport.
